title: Classifying magnons in itinerant ferromagnets from linear response TDDFT: Fe, Ni and Co revisited
abstract: The magnetic excitation spectrum of itinerant magnets exhibits rich and complex spectral features that often complicate interpretation of the underlying physics. For perturbations in the long wavelength limit, one obtains a well defined pole at zero frequency in the spectral function, the Goldstone magnon. However, for optical modes and finite wavevectors, the magnon spectrum may become damped, exhibit branching, or be completely washed out. In the present work, we show how the physical mechanism of all such features can be understood from careful analysis of the eigenmodes of the many-body spectral function. We perform first principles computations of elemental itinerant ferromagnets using a novel implementation of the linear response time-dependent density functional theory (LR-TDDFT) framework and classify the collective nature of individual spectral features based on the self-enhancement function, the product of the noninteracting Kohn-Sham susceptibility and the exchange-correlation kernel. In particular, we distinguish between coherent and incoherent collective excitations, depending on whether the real part of the self-enhancement function crosses unity at the spectral peak of the magnon, which may or may not be subject to Landau damping as quantified by the imaginary part. Classifying the computed magnon spectra accordingly, we observe coexistence of coherent magnon branches in bcc-Fe, as well as decoherence of the primary magnon branch in fcc-Ni for wave vectors near the BZ boundary where incoherent valley magnons instead carry substantial spectral weight. The analysis also naturally leads to a definition of the many-body Stoner spectrum and allows us to quantify the binding energy of the Stoner pair excitations.
summary: This paper presents a new way to accurately calculate and classify magnetic excitations (magnons) in metals like iron and nickel. By improving the numerical implementation of TDDFT, the authors successfully eliminate non-physical energy gaps and can distinguish between stable, coherent magnons and decaying, incoherent excitations. The work reveals complex phenomena such as the coexistence of multiple magnon branches and the existence of 'valley magnons' in nickel, providing a more precise theoretical benchmark for neutron scattering experiments.
main_problem: The difficulty in interpreting complex magnetic excitation spectra in itinerant ferromagnets, specifically distinguishing between coherent magnons and incoherent Stoner excitations, and resolving numerical 'Goldstone gap' errors in TDDFT.
main_result: The authors developed a novel LR-TDDFT implementation that eliminates the Goldstone gap error and provides a method to classify magnons as coherent or incoherent using the self-enhancement function, revealing features like branch coexistence in Fe and valley magnons in Ni.
method: A novel implementation of linear response time-dependent density functional theory (LR-TDDFT) within the PAW method, utilizing eigendecomposition of the spectral function and the self-enhancement function.
model_or_system: Elemental itinerant ferromagnets including bcc-Fe, fcc-Ni, fcc-Co, and hcp-Co, modeled using the Adiabatic Local Density Approximation (ALDA) within a TDDFT framework.
key_observables: Transverse magnetic susceptibility, scattering function, self-enhancement function, magnon dispersion, and Stoner continuum/gap.
important_parameters: Wave vectors (q), frequency (omega), exchange splitting, and plane-wave energy cutoffs.
main_assumptions: The use of the Adiabatic Local Density Approximation (ALDA) for the exchange-correlation kernel and the focus on collinear magnetic systems without spin-orbit coupling.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the physics of the self-enhancement function; Figure 2 shows the convergence of the Goldstone gap error; Figure 3 compares many-body vs. Kohn-Sham Stoner spectra; Figure 4 shows eigenvalues and magnon lineshapes for Co; Figure 14-15 show magnon enhancement and branch coexistence in Fe; Figure 16 shows the Ni spectrum; Figure 18-19 show convergence of peak frequencies.
paper_structure: Introduction to the problem of itinerant magnetism; description of the new LR-TDDFT implementation and error elimination strategy; application to Fe, Ni, and Co; classification of excitations via the self-enhancement function; discussion of convergence and basis set requirements; conclusion on implications for magnetic properties.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Cartoon of the physics encoded in the self- enhancement function (13). Illustrates how a change (with re- spect to the ground state) of the four-component electron den- sity (represented by the cloud) in the past, δnν(r′, t′), invokes fluctuations δnµ(r, t) in the future via the induced changes to the Hartree-exchange-correlation potential δW τ1 Hxc(r1, t1) in the interim, t′ ≤t1 ≤t. The wiggly line represents the kernel (11), while the bubble represents the Kohn-Sham susceptibil- ity (10).

FIG. 2. Goldstone gap error in Fe, Ni and Co as a function of the number of empty-shell bands per atom and inverse local-field cutoff. When varying the number of bands (left), the plane-wave cutoff is fixed to 3.6 keV for bcc and fcc structures and 2.4 keV for hcp-Co. When varying the plane-wave cutoff (right), the number of empty-shell bands per atom is fixed to 32.

FIG. 3. Total scattering function [trace of Eq. (42)] of Fe, Ni and Co at zero momentum transfer, plotted alongside the total scattering of the single-particle Kohn-Sham system. The Stoner shift indicates the redshift of the Stoner pair gap, using values from Tab. II.

FIG. 4. Eigenvalues s+− n (grey) of the scattering function (43) in fcc-Co (top) and hcp-Co (bottom) at zero momentum transfer for the many-body system (left) and Kohn-Sham system (right). For fcc/hcp Co, the 12/24 largest eigenvalues are plotted. In color, the projection (45) of the majority spectral function (44) onto the extracted collective eigenmodes |vn(qΓ)⟩is shown, lying virtually on top of the corresponding eigenvalue for a wide range of frequencies. The effective (Kohn-Sham) exchange splitting ∆KS and (many-body) Stoner pair gap ∆MB—see Tab. II—are shown as vertical dotted lines.

FIG. 5. Stoner spectrum [trace of Eq. (47)] of Fe, Ni and Co at zero momentum transfer, plotted alongside the corresponding Stoner spectrum of the noninteracting Kohn-Sham system. The vertical colored lines indicate the (many-body) Stoner pair gap ∆MB and the effective (Kohn-Sham) exchange splitting ∆KS, see Tab. II and Eq. (48).

FIG. 6. Magnon mode lineshape a+− 0 (q, ω) in fcc-Co (colored contour) overlaid by the corresponding Kohn-Sham lineshape a+− KS,0(q, ω) (greyscale line contour). Both contours are linear up to a+− 0 = 1 (atomic units), and logarithmic above. Coherent magnon peaks in the many-body lineshape are indicated with crosses, incoherent peaks with disks. The extracted peak positions are compared to inelastic scanning tunnel spectroscopy data (translucent squares) measured on a nine monolayer Co/Cu(100) film assuming isotropy of the dispersion [49].

FIG. 7. Collective magnon enhancement at qΓ in Co (fcc). On the left axis, the magnon lineshape a+− 0 (q, ω) (opaque blue) is compared to the singular basis function lineshape (50) (translucent blue) and the many-body Stoner spectrum [trace of Eq. (47)] (teal). For the Γ-point in particular, a+− sbf overlaps perfectly with a+−. On the right axis, the magnon mode projection of the self-enhancement function (24) is shown.
FIG. 8. Collective magnon enhancement at qX/2 (left) and qX (right) in Co (fcc).

FIG. 9. Magnon mode lineshape a+− n (q, ω) in hcp-Co (colored contour) overlaid by the corresponding Kohn-Sham lineshape a+− KS,n(q, ω) (greyscale line contour). Both contours are linear up to a+− 0 = 1 (atomic units), and logarithmic above. The top axis indicates the magnon mode, changing between n = 0 (acoustic) and n = 1 (optical) at each K and A point. Coherent magnon peaks in the many-body lineshape are indicated with crosses, incoherent peaks with disks. The extracted peak positions are compared to inelastic neutron scattering data (translucent squares) [50].

FIG. 10. Magnon mode lineshape a+− 0 (q, ω) in hcp-Co at the degenerate hexagonal face of the BZ boundary (colored contour) overlaid by the corresponding Kohn-Sham lineshape a+− KS,0(q, ω) (greyscale line contour) and extracted coherent magnon peak frequencies (blue crosses). For additional de- tails, please refer to Fig. 9.
